coredata.dc:title: State of health estimation of lithium-ion batteries based on a novel indirect health indicator
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: ICPE 2021 - The 2nd International Conference on Power Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Da
Zhang, Xuesong
Wang, Lulu
Zhao, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Accurately estimating the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries is necessary to ensure the battery system’s safe, stable, and efficient operation. It can be directly predicted by capacity, but the latter is difficult to measure online. Therefore, this paper first extracts new indirect health indicators from the voltage and current curves during charging and optimizes them using the Kalman filter. The Pearson correlation analysis method shows that the extracted HIs have a good correlation with the capacity. On this basis, Gaussian process regression (GPR) is modified into multi-kernel Gaussian process regression (MKGPR) by using the squared exponential covariance function and the periodic covariance function to refine the accurateness of SOH estimation. The hyper-parameters of the MKGPR model are solved employing particle swarm optimization (PSO) to reduce the errors caused by the artificial adjustment. Finally, the lithium-ion battery data set provided by NASA is used to evaluate the given method, and the experimental findings reveal that the proposed approach has high accuracy and stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Indirect health indicator
State of health
Multiple kernel Gaussian process regression
Particle swarm optimization

Results of SOH estimation of B0005.

RMSE and MAE of the PSO-MKGPR model.

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 2021 The 2nd International Conference on Power Engineering (ICPE 2021), December 09–11, 2021, Nanning, Guangxi, China

head.ce:title: State of health estimation of lithium-ion batteries based on a novel indirect health indicator

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Electric Power Research Institute State Grid Zhejiang Electric Power Co., Ltd, Hangzhou 310027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Electric Power Research Institute State Grid Zhejiang Electric Power Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Electric Power Research Institute State Grid Zhejiang Electric Power Co., Ltd, Hangzhou 310027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Renewable Energy Utilization and Energy Saving (Hefei University of Technology), Hefei 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Renewable Energy Utilization and Energy Saving (Hefei University of Technology)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Renewable Energy Utilization and Energy Saving (Hefei University of Technology), Hefei 230009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurately estimating the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries is necessary to ensure the battery system’s safe, stable, and efficient operation. It can be directly predicted by capacity, but the latter is difficult to measure online. Therefore, this paper first extracts new indirect health indicators from the voltage and current curves during charging and optimizes them using the Kalman filter. The Pearson correlation analysis method shows that the extracted HIs have a good correlation with the capacity. On this basis, Gaussian process regression (GPR) is modified into multi-kernel Gaussian process regression (MKGPR) by using the squared exponential covariance function and the periodic covariance function to refine the accurateness of SOH estimation. The hyper-parameters of the MKGPR model are solved employing particle swarm optimization (PSO) to reduce the errors caused by the artificial adjustment. Finally, the lithium-ion battery data set provided by NASA is used to evaluate the given method, and the experimental findings reveal that the proposed approach has high accuracy and stability.

ce:section.ce:para: The lithium-ion battery is essential for storing and using clean energy because of its lightweight, good stability, no memory effect, and high energy density [1]. However, with the increase of charging and discharging times, lithium-ion batteries will age due to various physical and chemical mechanisms, which will affect the regular operation of electrical equipment and even lead to catastrophic accidents. To assure the battery’s secure, robust, and efficient operation, it is necessary to accurately diagnose the SOH of the lithium-ion battery [2].

Model-based methods and data-driven methods are the two types of SOH prediction methods currently available. [3]. The model-based methods are mainly based on the equivalent circuit model [4] and electrochemical mechanism model [5] to construct the mathematical degradation model of lithium-ion batteries. However, the prediction accuracy of this method is closely related to the complexity of the model. Because of the high nonlinearity of the battery system, building a model with great precision is challenging. The data-driven method does not involve a specific reaction mechanism, and its primary task is to extract feature values of model training. Feature extraction can obtain meaningful information from the original data without affecting the model’s performance and simplify the training process. In [6], eight indirect health indicators, such as the lowest point of discharge voltage and the charging time, are obtained from the voltage, temperature, and current curves during charging and discharging. For SOH estimation, the Ref. [7] capture the time period and the temperature period of an identical discharging voltage and time difference as HIs. However, compared with the discharge process that is highly dependent on the load, the battery management system can usually control the charging process, which is more steady and easier to assess since it is remarkably static. All Refs. [6,7] use the discharge process, which is not conducive to the practical application of the model. In addition, Li-ion batteries may not undergo a complete charging and discharging cycle in actual work, so it is more suitable to use segmented data in the feature extraction process. For example, literature [8] extracts the duration of a specific charging voltage segment as a health indicator. Furthermore, employing partial data can minimize computation costs while also addressing the issue of information overload.

After obtaining the features, the corresponding artificial intelligence algorithm is selected to train the SOH prediction model. In [9], the XGBoost algorithm is used to estimate the SOH, and the Markov chain is used to correct the predicted value. Literature [10] combines LSTM with PSO and attention mechanism to predict SOH of lithium-ion batteries. These data-driven methods can give satisfactory estimation accuracy, but the estimation results can only provide point prediction results without uncertainty expression ability. In practical application, the uncertainty of the estimated value is helpful for us to make a better choice. Gaussian process regression (GPR) is a probability prediction model in the Bayesian framework, which can solve nonlinear regression problems with fewer data. In [11], the SOH prediction is achieved using the GPR model, which has a great prediction performance. Moreover, the setting of hyper-parameters of the machine learning model has a significant influence on the prediction results. Therefore, the data-driven method usually needs to be combined with other optimization algorithms to determine the model’s hyper-parameters, reduce the errors caused by manual intervention and improve the long-term prediction performance [12].

We present a multi-kernel GPR model which considers the indirect health indicators of voltage and current data during charging in this essay and adjusts parameters by PSO algorithm to achieve the prediction of SOH of lithium-ion batteries. Firstly, indirect health indicators are extracted from the charging voltage curve and charging current curve. Pearson correlation coefficient is devoted to analyzing the relationship between the extracted HIs and capacity quantitatively. Kalman filter is used to filter and optimize the extracted HIs to improve the correlation further. Then, the GPR model based on the combined kernel function is used to model the aging process of Li-ion batteries to realize SOH estimation. PSO is applied to refine the important parameters and solve the problem of parameter selection. Finally, the feasibility and effectiveness of this method are verified and tested by NASA battery data. The results of the experiments reveal that this strategy is highly accurate and useful.

The remainder of this work is laid out as follows. Section 2 describes the data set used and the process of feature extraction and optimization. Section 3 introduces the MKGPR model and parameter adjustment process of PSO. The experimental results are verified and analyzed in Section 4. Ultimately, Section 5 discusses the conclusion.

The data on battery aging utilized in this paper is from the lithium-ion battery data set NASA Ames Center for Excellence in Prediction (PCoE) [13]. In this paper, HIs are extracted from the charging process of batteries. The charging process of this data set is as follows. At first, the charging is done in a 1.5 A constant current mode, and after the voltage rises to 4.2 V, it is charged at a steady voltage until the charging current lowers to 20 mA.

The coefficient of variation [14] is a normalized measure of the dispersion degree of the probability distribution, which is used to compare the dispersion degree of different sample data. It is defined as:
(1)
c
v a r

μ
=
∑

N
(

i
− μ )
2
N
μ
where
c
v a r
is the discrete coefficient, σ is the standard deviation of samples, μ is the average value of samples, N is the sample size. As a feature, the coefficient of variation is introduced into the current curve during constant voltage charging (Cvar_CV_C) and the voltage curve during constant current charging (Cvar_CC_V).

Different HIs have different trends, so it is difficult to judge the correlation between HI and capacity directly. To quantitatively analyze the correlation between features and capacity, the Pearson correlation coefficient is introduced. The calculation formula is as follows:
(2)
Pearson =
E
A B
− E
A
E
( B )
E
A
2
−
E
2
( A )
E
B
2
−
E
2
( B )
where A and B represent the totality of feature and capacity samples. The higher similarity between two variables, the higher the absolute value of the correlation coefficient.

According to formula (2), the correlation coefficient between the coefficient of variation characteristics and capacity in different intervals is calculated. Finally, the optimal voltage range with the highest correlation is [3.8 V–4.2 V], and the optimal current range is [0.3 A–1.0 A]. The correlation coefficient between HIs and capacity in this range is shown in Table 1.

Compared with the original capacity curve, the extracted HI has some noise pollution, which will inevitably affect the final forecast accuracy. Therefore, this study selects the Kalman filter [15] to filter the extracted HIs data. Fig. 1 is the curve of filtered HIs and capacity, and the correlation coefficient between optimized HIs and capacity is shown in Table 2. It can be seen that the filtered features are closer to the original capacity curve, and the correlation coefficient is improved.

Generally, a zero mean function is used in the GPR model, which can be obtained by preprocessing data. There are many options for covariance function, but as for the capacity curve, the GPR model using only a single covariance function cannot meet the prediction requirements because of the capacity regeneration phenomenon. In this paper, the capacity deterioration is described using the squared exponential covariance function, and the regeneration effect is reduced using the periodic covariance function. The combination of these two functions can accurately predict the capacity degradation curve of lithium-ion batteries. At this time, the hyper-parameters are
θ =

)

To optimize hyper-parameters, the traditional way is to use the Minimize Negative Log-Likelihood (NLML) function. However, this method relies too much on the initial value and easily falls into the local optimum. To overcome the above shortcomings, this paper adopts the PSO algorithm to solve the hyper-parameters.

In this paper, PSO [16] is accustomed to automatically searching the optimal parameters of the MKGPR model in the process of training, and the PSO-MKGPR model is established. The algorithm flowchart is shown in Fig. 2.
The overall framework of this paper is shown in Fig. 3.

The mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) are used as evaluation indexes to validate the performance of the suggested approach, and the calculation formula is:
(14)
MAE =
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
|
y
i
−
y
i
̂
|
(15)
RMSE =
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
y
i
−
y
i
̂
2

To ensure that the proposed model is accurate and reliable, the model was tested on four lithium-ion batteries, B0005, B0006, B0007, and B0018, and the prediction was made at different starting points and the prediction results were compared to verify the generalization ability of the model. Figs. 4–7 show the prediction results of SOH for the B0005, B0006, B0007, and B0018 batteries at three different prediction starting points (SP).

As can be seen in the diagram, the real values of the four batteries are very close to the predicted values. The estimated value of the suggested approach can flawlessly forecast capacity degradation and accurately capture the phenomenon of capacity regeneration. The GPR model can give the estimated value of SOH and calculate the confidence interval of the estimated value of SOH. The shaded part in the figure indicates the 95% confidence interval of the estimated value, indicating that the real value has a 95% probability of appearing in this interval. Generally, the narrower the confidence interval, the higher the reliability of the model. The confidence interval of the proposed model is narrow, and the reliability of the model is high. Table 3 shows the prediction error of SOH. It can be seen that the MAE and RMSE of the proposed model are less than 1% at the three prediction starting points of battery B0005 and battery B0007, and the capacity regeneration phenomenon of battery B0006 and battery B0018 is too severe, resulting in a significant prediction error. Still, the overall error is less than 2%. It indicates the recommended approach in this paper has high accuracy and reliability. Tests on different prediction starting points show that although there are significant differences in prediction starting points, the difference in SOH prediction performance is tiny. As a result, the suggested technique is less affected by forecast starting locations. The proposed model has a strong generalization ability.

This paper proposes a prediction framework for the SOH of lithium-ion batteries based on the PSO-MKGPR model. The coefficients of variation of voltage and current curves during the partial charging stage are extracted as HIs, and the Pearson correlation coefficient verifies the validity of the selected indicators. Then HIs are optimized by the Kalman filter. With HIs as input and capacity as output, the battery aging model is obtained by training in the PSO-MKGPR model. Finally, the HIs data extracted online are sent to the trained PSO-MKGPR model to realize SOH prediction. The results of the experiments reveal that this strategy has a high prediction precision. Changing the prediction starting point has almost no bias on the prediction results, indicating that the model has high generalization ability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Research on Safety Analysis and Fault Warning of Energy Storage Power Station Based on Knowledge Graph, Science and Technology Project of State Grid Zhejiang Electric Power Co., Ltd, China (No. 5211DS19002D).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Birkl, C. R., Roberts, M. R., McTurk, E., Bruce, P. G., & Howey, D. A. (2017). Degradation diagnostics for lithium-ion cells. Journal of Power Sources, 341, 373-386.ref-001

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery state of health estimation: a structured review of models, methods and commercial devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ungurean, L., Cârstoiu, G., Micea, M. V., & Groza, V. (2017). Battery state of health estimation: a structured review of models, methods and commercial devices. International Journal of Energy Research, 41(2), 151-181.ref-002

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of data-driven state of health estimation for lithium-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xian, Z., Guang, G., Da, L., Zhi, L., Yang. Z. (2021). Review of Data-driven State of Health Estimation for Lithium-ion Battery. ZheJiang Electric Power, 40(07), 65-73.ref-003

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comparative study of equivalent circuit models for Li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, X., Li, S., & Peng, H. (2012). A comparative study of equivalent circuit models for Li-ion batteries. Journal of Power Sources, 198, 359-367.ref-004

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A lead–acid battery’s remaining useful life prediction by using electrochemical model in the particle filtering framework

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lyu, C., Lai, Q., Ge, T., Yu, H., Wang, L., & Ma, N. (2017). A lead–acid battery’s remaining useful life prediction by using electrochemical model in the Particle Filtering framework. Energy, 120, 975-984.ref-005

sb:title.sb:maintitle: SOH and RUL prediction of lithium-ion batteries based on gaussian process regression with indirect health indicators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jia, J., Liang, J., Shi, Y., Wen, J., Pang, X., & Zeng, J. (2020). SOH and RUL prediction of lithium-ion batteries based on gaussian process regression with indirect health indicators. Energies, 13(2), 375.ref-006

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State of health prediction of lithium-ion batteries based on the discharge voltage and temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, Y., Wen, J., Shi, Y., & Zeng, J. (2021). State of Health Prediction of Lithium-Ion Batteries Based on the Discharge Voltage and Temperature. Electronics, 10(12), 1497.ref-007

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ensemble learning-based data-driven method for online state-of-health estimation of lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gou, B., Xu, Y., & Feng, X. (2020). An Ensemble Learning-based Data-Driven Method for Online State-of-Health Estimation of Lithium-ion Batteries. IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification, 7(2), 422-436.ref-008

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery SOH estimation based on XGBoost algorithm with accuracy correction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Song, S., Fei, C., & Xia, H. (2020). Lithium-ion battery SOH estimation based on XGBoost algorithm with accuracy correction. Energies, 13(4), 812.ref-009

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A neural-network-based method for RUL prediction and SOH monitoring of lithium-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Qu, J., Liu, F., Ma, Y., & Fan, J. (2019). A neural-network-based method for RUL prediction and SOH monitoring of lithium-ion battery. IEEE access, 7, 87178-87191.ref-010

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A joint state of health and remaining useful life estimation approach for lithium-ion batteries based on health factor parameter

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, P., Fan L., & Cheng, Z. (2021). A Joint State of Health and Remaining Useful Life Estimation Approach for Lithium-ion Batteries Based on Health Factor Parameter. Proceedings of the CSEE, https://doi.org/10.13334/j.0258-8013.pcsee.202368.ref-011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On hyperparameter optimization of machine learning algorithms: Theory and practice

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, L., & Shami, A. (2020). On hyperparameter optimization of machine learning algorithms: Theory and practice. Neurocomputing, 415, 295-316.ref-012
Sahaand, B., & Goebel, K. (2007). Battery Data Set, NASA ames prognostics data repository. NASA Ames Research Center.ref-013

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monitoring the coefficient of variation: A literature review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jalilibal, Z., Amiri, A., Castagliola, P., & Khoo, M. B. (2021). Monitoring the Coefficient of Variation: A Literature Review. Computers & Industrial Engineering, 107600.ref-014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel framework for lithium-ion battery state of charge estimation based on Kalman filter Gaussian process regression

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, X., Chen, X., & Chen, X. (2021). A novel framework for lithium-ion battery state of charge estimation based on Kalman filter Gaussian process regression. International Journal of Energy Research, 45(9), 13238-13249.ref-015
Poli, R., Kennedy, J., & Blackwell, T. (2007). Particle swarm optimization. Swarm intelligence, 1(1), 33-57.ref-016
